text:
Aussie sports stars and celebrities have jumped to the support of breaking athlete and viral internet sensation Rachael 'Raygun' Gunn after a barrage of ridicule online.

Australian breaking athlete and viral internet sensation Rachael ‘Raygun’ Gunn has drawn an outpouring of support from prominent Aussie sports stars and celebrities after her disastrous performance at the 2024 Paris Olympic Games became the subject of widespread ridicule online.

Gunn, a 36-year-old university lecturer, on Friday lost all three pool matches against the USA, France and Lithuania in the sport’s opener at Paris’ Place de la Concorde.

Fans onsocial media slammed the Australian representativefor her clumsy roll and wriggle on the floor as she failed to qualify for the quarter finals on Saturday.

Enthusiasts also mocked Gunn’s Australian Olympic tracksuit as her choice of uniform, a stark contrast to her competitor’s hip streetwear styles.

Many labelled her performance “disgraceful” and an “embarrassment” for Australians, as some likened it to the dancing of a young child.

The barrage of criticism inspired former Socceroo-turned-commentator Craig Foster to demand Gunn be given an Order of Australia medal, one of the nation’s highest honours.

“Dr Raygun is an Aussie legend,” he wrote on X, formerly Twitter.

“We dance to our own beat. And bring marsupial energy to every contest. She’s a national treasure. Give her an AO.”

Dr Raygun is an Aussie legend.We dance to our own beat.And bring marsupial energy to every contest.Sheâ€™s a national treasure.Give her an AO.ðŸ’šðŸ’›#Paris2024pic.twitter.com/yaTaF5LTSz

The now instantly recognisable B-Girl also drew support from Aussie comedy duo The Inspired Unemployed who posted a video of the pair dancing down a Paris street with her less than a day after her Olympic flop became an internet sensation.

“The new Queen of Australia,” the video’s caption read.

The post was met with a crown emoji from Australian men’s cricket captain Pat Cummins while satirical news site ‘The Betoota Advocate’ wrote: “The Olympic arena can’t handle this street s**t.”

Anna Meares, former Olympic cyclist and Australian chef de mission, jumped to Gunn’s defence in one of her final press conferences at the Paris Games.

“I haven’t spoken to Rachael yet but what I can say is that I love Rachael and I think that what has occurred on social media with trolls and keyboard warriors and taking those comments and giving them airtime has been really disappointing,” Ms Meares said.

The Australian Olympics head continued to speak on the struggles Gunn faced as she was breaking her way into the typically male sport.

"In 2008, she was locked in a room crying, being involved in a male-dominated sport as the only woman," Ms Meares said.

"And it took great courage for her to continue on and fight for her opportunity to participate in a sport that she loved.

"That got her to winning the Olympic qualifying event to be here in Paris.

"She is the best breakdancer - female - that we have for Australia."

Gunn's male counterpart, 16-year-old Jeff Dunne, praised his fellow Aussie breaker after her match-up.

“All I know is that she represented hard,” he said. “She’s leading breaking in Australia for the women, and I just acknowledge her and respect her 100 per cent.”

Following the initial barrage of internet trolling, Gunn hit back at those who mocked her choice of uniform.

“Looking forward to the same level of scrutiny on what the B-boys wear tomorrow,” she wrote in an Instagram story.

She went on to share a post on the importance of individuality following widespread backlash to her being knocked out in the pool round.

“Don’t be afraid to be different,” the post read.

“Go out there and represent yourself, you never know where that’s gonna take you.”

Gunn is Australia's top-ranking female breaker and a creative arts researcher specialising in the cultural politics of breakdancing at Sydney's Macquarie University.

She secured her place in the Games as Australia’s B-Girl delegate after winning last year’s Oceania Breaking Championships against a dancer named Molly.

Speaking to reporters after her Paris flop failed to maintain her status as a breaking great, Gunn admitted she was never going to win in 2024.

“All my moves are original,” she said.

“I was never going to beat these girls on what they do best, the dynamic and the power moves, so I wanted to move differently, be artistic and creative because how many chances do you get in a lifetime to do that on an international stage?

“I was always the underdog and wanted to make my mark in a different way.”
